content:
# Luke Chapter 12 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 102-104 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/LUK_012_page_001.jpg, LUK_012_page_002.jpg, LUK_012_page_003.jpg

---

**1** In the meantime, when the myriads of the crowd had gathered together, so that they trampled upon one another, he began to say to his disciples first, "Beware of the leaven of the Pharisees, which is hypocrisy.

**2** But there is nothing covered up that shall not be revealed, and hidden that shall not be made known.

**3** Therefore whatever things you have said in the darkness shall be heard in the light, and what you have spoken to the ear in the inner rooms shall be proclaimed upon the housetops.

**4** And I say to you, my friends, do not fear those who kill the body and after that have nothing more that they can do.

**5** But I will show you whom you should fear: fear the one who, after killing, has authority to cast into Gehenna. Yes, I say to you, fear this one.

**6** Are not five sparrows sold for two assaria? And not one of them is forgotten before God.

**7** But even the hairs of your head are all numbered. Do not fear; you are of more value than many sparrows.

**8** And I say to you, everyone who confesses me before men, the Son of Man also shall confess him before the angels of God.

**9** But the one who denies me before men shall be denied before the angels of God.

**10** And everyone who shall speak a word against the Son of Man, it shall be forgiven him; but to the one who blasphemes against the Holy Spirit, it shall not be forgiven.

**11** And when they bring you before the synagogues and the rulers and the authorities, do not be anxious how or what you shall answer or what you shall say;

**12** for the Holy Spirit shall teach you in that very hour what it is needful to say.

**13** And someone from the crowd said to him, "Teacher, tell my brother to divide the inheritance with me."

**14** But he said to him, "Man, who appointed me a judge or a divider over you?"

**15** And he said to them, "Take heed and guard yourselves from all covetousness, for not in the abundance of one's possessions does his life consist."

**16** And he spoke a parable to them, saying, "The land of a certain rich man brought forth plentifully.

**17** And he was reasoning within himself, saying, 'What shall I do, for I have no place where I shall store my crops?'

**18** And he said, 'This I will do: I will tear down my barns and build greater ones, and I will store there all my grain and my goods.

**19** And I will say to my soul, "Soul, you have many goods laid up for many years; rest, eat, drink, be merry."'

**20** But God said to him, 'Fool! This night your soul is required of you; and the things you have prepared, whose shall they be?'

**21** So is the one who stores up treasure for himself and is not rich toward God."

**22** And he said to his disciples, "Therefore I say to you, do not be anxious for your soul, what you shall eat, nor for your body, what you shall put on.

**23** For the soul is more than food, and the body more than clothing.

**24** Consider the ravens, that they neither sow nor reap, which have neither storehouse nor barn, and God feeds them. How much more are you of value than the birds!

**25** And which of you by being anxious is able to add a cubit to his stature?

**26** If therefore you are not able to do even the least thing, why are you anxious about the rest?

**27** Consider the lilies, how they grow: they neither toil nor spin; yet I say to you, not even Solomon in all his glory was arrayed as one of these.

**28** But if God so clothes the grass in the field, which today exists and tomorrow is cast into the oven, how much more shall he clothe you, O you of little faith!

**29** And you, do not seek what you shall eat and what you shall drink, and do not be of anxious mind.

**30** For all these things the nations of the world seek after; but your Father knows that you have need of these things.

**31** But seek his kingdom, and these things shall be added to you.

**32** Do not fear, little flock, for it was your Father's good pleasure to give you the kingdom.

**33** Sell your possessions and give alms. Make for yourselves purses that do not grow old, an unfailing treasure in the heavens, where no thief comes near nor moth destroys.

**34** For where your treasure is, there also shall your heart be.

**35** Let your loins be girded and your lamps burning,

**36** and you yourselves be like men who are waiting for their lord, when he shall return from the wedding feast, that when he comes and knocks they may open to him immediately.

**37** Blessed are those servants whom the lord, when he comes, shall find watching. Truly I say to you, he shall gird himself and have them recline at table, and having come he shall serve them.

**38** And if he shall come in the second watch, or in the third watch, and find them so, blessed are those servants.

**39** But know this, that if the master of the house had known at what hour the thief was coming, he would not have allowed his house to be broken into.

**40** You also, be ready, for at an hour you do not expect, the Son of Man comes."

**41** And Peter said, "Lord, do you speak this parable to us, or also to all?"

**42** And the Lord said, "Who then is the faithful steward, the wise one, whom the lord shall set over his household servants, to give them their portion of food at the proper time?

**43** Blessed is that servant whom his lord, when he comes, shall find so doing.

**44** Truly I say to you, he shall set him over all his possessions.

**45** But if that servant should say in his heart, 'My lord delays in coming,' and should begin to beat the menservants and the maidservants, and to eat and drink and be drunk,

**46** the lord of that servant shall come on a day when he does not expect and at an hour which he does not know, and shall cut him asunder and appoint his portion with the unfaithful.

**47** And that servant who knew the will of his lord, and did not prepare nor do according to his will, shall be beaten with many stripes.

**48** But the one who did not know, yet did things worthy of stripes, shall be beaten with few. And to everyone to whom much was given, much shall be required of him; and to whom they entrusted much, of him they shall ask the more.

**49** I came to cast fire upon the earth, and how I wish it were already kindled!

**50** But I have a baptism to be baptized with, and how I am constrained until it is accomplished!

**51** Do you think that I came to give peace on the earth? No, I say to you, but rather division.

**52** For from now on there shall be five in one house divided, three against two and two against three.

**53** They shall be divided, father against son and son against father, mother against daughter and daughter against mother, mother-in-law against her daughter-in-law and daughter-in-law against mother-in-law."

**54** And he said also to the crowds, "When you see a cloud rising from the west, immediately you say, 'A rainstorm is coming,' and so it happens.

**55** And when a south wind is blowing, you say, 'There will be scorching heat,' and it happens.

**56** Hypocrites! You know how to discern the face of the earth and of the heaven, but how is it you do not know how to discern this present time?

**57** And why do you not even of yourselves judge what is right?

**58** For as you are going with your adversary before a magistrate, on the way give diligence to be released from him, lest he drag you to the judge, and the judge deliver you to the officer, and the officer cast you into prison.

**59** I say to you, you shall by no means come out from there until you have paid the very last lepton."

---

## SOPHIA's Council Notes

### Manuscript Verification

The three Sinaiticus manuscript images (pages 102-104) were examined. Page 1 covers approximately Luke 11:33 through Luke 12:6 across four columns, with Luke 12:1 beginning at the rightmost column. Page 2 covers approximately Luke 12:7 through Luke 12:30 across four columns. Page 3 covers approximately Luke 12:46 through Luke 13:2 across four columns. The text is in Sinaiticus's characteristic continuous uncial script, four columns per page. Key readings confirmed from the manuscript:

- ΤΩΝ ΜΥΡΙΑΔΩΝ ΤΟΥ ΟΧΛΟΥ (12:1, "the myriads of the crowd" — hyperbolic multitude)
- ΗΤΙΣ ΕΣΤΙΝ ΥΠΟΚΡΙΣΙΣ (12:1, "which is hypocrisy" — explicit identification of the leaven)
- ΕΞΟΥΣΙΑΝ ΕΧΟΝΤΑ ΜΕΤΑ ΤΟ ΑΠΟΚΤΕΙΝΑΙ ΕΜΒΑΛΕΙΝ ΕΙΣ ΤΗΝ ΓΕΕΝΝΑΝ (12:5, "having authority after the killing to cast into Gehenna")
- ΑΣΣΑΡΙΩΝ ΔΥΟ (12:6, "two assaria" — small Roman copper coins)
- ΟΜΟΛΟΓΗΣΗ ΕΝ ΕΜΟΙ (12:8, "shall confess in me" — Semitic idiom)
- ΕΙΣ ΤΟ ΑΓΙΟΝ ΠΝΕΥΜΑ ΒΛΑΣΦΗΜΗΣΑΝΤΙ (12:10, blasphemy against the Holy Spirit)
- ΚΡΙΤΗΝ Η ΜΕΡΙΣΤΗΝ (12:14, "judge or divider" — *meristes* unique in NT)
- ΠΑΣΗΣ ΠΛΕΟΝΕΞΙΑΣ (12:15, "all covetousness")
- ΕΥΦΟΡΗΣΕΝ (12:16, "brought forth plentifully" — NT hapax)
- ΑΦΡΩΝ (12:20, "Fool" — the moral fool of Wisdom literature)
- ΑΠΑΙΤΟΥΣΙΝ (12:20, "they require" — impersonal plural for divine action)
- ΕΙΣ ΘΕΟΝ ΠΛΟΥΤΩΝ (12:21, "being rich toward God" — unique to Luke)
- ΜΗ ΜΕΤΕΩΡΙΖΕΣΘΕ (12:29, "do not be of anxious mind" — NT hapax)
- ΤΟ ΜΙΚΡΟΝ ΠΟΙΜΝΙΟΝ (12:32, "the little flock" — diminutive, unique in Gospels)
- ΒΑΛΛΑΝΤΙΑ ΜΗ ΠΑΛΑΙΟΥΜΕΝΑ (12:33, "purses that do not grow old")
- ΘΗΣΑΥΡΟΝ ΑΝΕΚΛΕΙΠΤΟΝ (12:33, "unfailing treasure" — NT hapax)
- ΣΙΤΟΜΕΤΡΙΟΝ (12:42, "portion of food/grain ration" — NT hapax)
- ΔΙΧΟΤΟΜΗΣΕΙ (12:46, "shall cut asunder")
- ΜΕΤΑ ΤΩΝ ΑΠΙΣΤΩΝ (12:46, "with the unfaithful" — where Matthew reads "hypocrites")
- ΔΙΑΜΕΡΙΣΜΟΝ (12:51, "division" — where Matthew reads "sword"; NT hapax)
- ΥΠΟΚΡΙΤΑΙ (12:56, "Hypocrites" — forming inclusio with 12:1)
- ΠΡΑΚΤΩΡ (12:58, "officer/bailiff" — NT hapax, distinctly Lukan)
- ΛΕΠΤΟΝ (12:59, "lepton" — smallest coin in circulation)

### Translation Principles Applied

1. **12:1 — "In the meantime" vs. "In which time":** EMET renders "In which [time]," LOGOS "In the meantime," PNEUMA "Meanwhile." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "In the meantime" smoothly conveys the temporal connective while maintaining formal register; EMET is overly bracketed, PNEUMA too casual.

2. **12:1 — "Beware" vs. "Guard yourselves":** EMET renders "Guard yourselves from," LOGOS "Beware of," PNEUMA "Be on your guard against." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "Beware of" is the concise formal equivalent of *prosechete heautois apo*; EMET is more literal but cumbersome, PNEUMA expands unnecessarily.

3. **12:4 — "after that have nothing more that they can do" vs. "after these things not having anything more to do":** EMET renders "after these things not having anything more to do," LOGOS "after this have nothing more that they can do," PNEUMA "after that can do nothing more." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — clear and precise without EMET's participle-heavy construction.

4. **12:8 — "confesses me" vs. "confess in me":** EMET renders "confess in me" (preserving the Semitic *en emoi*), LOGOS "confesses me," PNEUMA "acknowledges me." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "confesses me" is natural English while retaining the theological weight; "confess in me" (EMET) is a Semitism that obscures meaning for English readers; "acknowledges" (PNEUMA) weakens the confessional force.

5. **12:12 — "needful" vs. "necessary" vs. "need":** EMET renders "it is necessary," LOGOS "it is needful," PNEUMA "you need." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "needful" maintains the elevated register consistent with SOPHIA's prior decision in 10:42 and 11:42.

6. **12:14 — "Man" vs. "Friend":** EMET and LOGOS render "Man" (translating the vocative *anthrope*), PNEUMA renders "Friend." SOPHIA follows EMET and LOGOS (2-to-1) — "Man" is the direct vocative address; "Friend" (PNEUMA) softens the Greek and imports a familiarity not present in the text.

7. **12:15 — "covetousness" vs. "greed":** EMET renders "covetousness," LOGOS "covetousness," PNEUMA "greed." SOPHIA follows EMET and LOGOS (2-to-1) — "covetousness" is the established theological equivalent of *pleonexia*, connecting to the Decalogue tradition.

8. **12:15 — "not in the abundance... does his life consist" vs. "not in the abounding... is his life out of his possessions":** EMET's rendering is awkward ("out of his possessions"); LOGOS provides a clearer formal equivalent ("not in the abundance of one's possessions does his life consist"); PNEUMA paraphrases freely. SOPHIA follows LOGOS for clarity and precision.

9. **12:16 — "brought forth plentifully" vs. "bore-well" vs. "produced an exceptional harvest":** EMET renders "bore-well," LOGOS "brought forth plentifully," PNEUMA "produced an exceptional harvest." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "brought forth plentifully" accurately renders *euphoresen* in natural English; "bore-well" (EMET) is awkwardly hyphenated; PNEUMA expands beyond the single verb.

10. **12:17 — "crops" vs. "fruits":** EMET renders "fruits" (*tous karpous*), LOGOS and PNEUMA render "crops." SOPHIA follows LOGOS and PNEUMA (2-to-1) — "crops" is the agricultural equivalent in context; "fruits" is technically literal but misleading in English.

11. **12:20 — "Fool" vs. "Senseless one":** EMET renders "Senseless [one]," LOGOS "Fool," PNEUMA "You fool." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "Fool" is the direct, forceful rendering of *aphron*, consistent with SOPHIA's decision in 11:40. The vocative demands directness.

12. **12:20 — "your soul is required of you" vs. "they demand your soul from you":** EMET renders "they demand your soul from you," LOGOS "your soul is required of you," PNEUMA "your life is being demanded back from you." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — the passive construction "is required" appropriately conveys the impersonal divine passive while maintaining formal dignity; PNEUMA's expansion ("demanded back") adds interpretation.

13. **12:21 — "stores up treasure" vs. "treasuring-up":** EMET renders "treasuring-up," LOGOS "stores up treasure," PNEUMA "stockpiles wealth." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — natural English prose over EMET's hyphenated participle and PNEUMA's loaded paraphrase.

14. **12:25 — "stature" vs. "life":** EMET renders "stature," LOGOS "stature," PNEUMA "life" (rendering *helikian* as lifespan). SOPHIA follows EMET and LOGOS (2-to-1) — "stature" is the more literal rendering of *helikian* with *pechyn* ("cubit"), preserving the ambiguity the Greek contains. The reader may discern either sense.

15. **12:27 — "lilies" vs. "wildflowers":** EMET and LOGOS render "lilies" (*ta krina*), PNEUMA renders "wildflowers." SOPHIA follows EMET and LOGOS (2-to-1) — "lilies" is the established rendering of *krina*; PNEUMA's "wildflowers" is botanically interpretive.

16. **12:27 — "was arrayed" vs. "clothed himself":** EMET renders "clothed himself," LOGOS "was arrayed," PNEUMA "was dressed." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "arrayed" carries the formal dignity appropriate to Solomon's royal splendor and is the traditional rendering.

17. **12:28 — "O you of little faith" vs. "ones of-little-faith":** EMET renders "[ones] of-little-faith," LOGOS "O you of little faith," PNEUMA "What little faith you have!" SOPHIA follows LOGOS — the vocative "O" captures the direct address of *oligopistoi* with appropriate solemnity; PNEUMA's exclamation is too colloquial.

18. **12:29 — "do not be of anxious mind" vs. "do not be anxious":** EMET renders "do not be anxious," LOGOS "do not be of anxious mind," PNEUMA "stop being anxious." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "of anxious mind" distinguishes *meteorizesthe* (the vivid hapax meaning "to be suspended in anxious uncertainty") from the simpler *merimnao* used elsewhere; EMET flattens this distinction; PNEUMA's "stop being" imports progressive aspect.

19. **12:31 — "But seek" vs. "Nevertheless, seek":** EMET renders "Nevertheless, seek," LOGOS "But seek," PNEUMA "Instead, seek." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "But" renders the adversative *plen* concisely; "Nevertheless" (EMET) is heavy; "Instead" (PNEUMA) is too informal.

20. **12:32 — "good pleasure" vs. "was well-pleased" vs. "has gladly chosen":** EMET renders "was well-pleased," LOGOS "it was your Father's good pleasure," PNEUMA "your Father has gladly chosen." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "good pleasure" preserves *eudokesen* with theological dignity; PNEUMA's "gladly chosen" interprets beyond the verb.

21. **12:35 — "Let your loins be girded" vs. "Stay dressed and ready for action":** EMET renders "Let your loins be girded," LOGOS "Let your loins be girded," PNEUMA "Stay dressed and ready for action." SOPHIA follows EMET and LOGOS (2-to-1) — the formal periphrastic construction preserves the Exodus 12:11 allusion; PNEUMA's paraphrase loses the biblical imagery.

22. **12:36 — "wedding feast" vs. "wedding-feasts" vs. "wedding banquet":** EMET renders "wedding-feasts" (plural), LOGOS "wedding feast," PNEUMA "wedding banquet." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — singular "wedding feast" is natural English; EMET's plural is awkward; PNEUMA's "banquet" adds interpretation.

23. **12:37 — "servants" vs. "slaves":** EMET renders "slaves" (*douloi*), LOGOS "servants," PNEUMA "servants." SOPHIA follows LOGOS and PNEUMA (2-to-1) — "servants" is the established consensus rendering in this parabolic context, consistent with SOPHIA's approach in prior chapters.

24. **12:38 — "or" vs. "and":** EMET renders "and in the third watch," LOGOS "and if in the third watch," PNEUMA paraphrases freely. SOPHIA renders "or in the third watch" — the Greek *kai en* here carries a disjunctive sense ("whether... or"), indicating alternative timing.

25. **12:39 — "broken into" vs. "dug-through":** EMET renders "dug-through" (literal *dioruchthēnai*), LOGOS "broken into," PNEUMA "broken into." SOPHIA follows LOGOS and PNEUMA (2-to-1) — "broken into" communicates the meaning clearly; "dug-through" (EMET) preserves the mud-brick construction detail but is unnatural in English.

26. **12:42 — "steward" vs. "manager":** EMET renders "steward," LOGOS "steward," PNEUMA "manager." SOPHIA follows EMET and LOGOS (2-to-1) — "steward" is the established rendering of *oikonomos*, preserving the theological weight the term carries through Luke 16.

27. **12:42 — "portion of food" vs. "portion-of-grain" vs. "food allowance":** EMET renders "portion-of-grain," LOGOS "portion of food," PNEUMA "food allowance." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "portion of food" renders *sitometrion* clearly without EMET's hyphenation or PNEUMA's modern idiom.

28. **12:45 — "menservants and maidservants" vs. "male-servants and female-servants":** EMET renders "male-servants and female-servants," LOGOS "menservants and maidservants," PNEUMA "servants, both men and women." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "menservants and maidservants" renders *paidas kai tas paidiskas* with appropriate formal distinction.

29. **12:46 — "cut him asunder" vs. "cut-him-asunder" vs. "cut him to pieces":** EMET renders "cut-him-asunder" (hyphenated), LOGOS "cut him asunder," PNEUMA "cut him to pieces." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "cut him asunder" preserves the severity of *dichotomēsei* without EMET's hyphenation or PNEUMA's expansion ("to pieces" overstates the Greek "in two").

30. **12:47 — "stripes" vs. "blows":** EMET renders "blows," LOGOS "stripes," PNEUMA "beating." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "stripes" is the formal, traditional rendering that carries the weight of the original; "blows" (EMET) is adequate but less precise; "beating" (PNEUMA) is too general.

31. **12:49 — "how I wish" vs. "what do I wish if":** EMET renders "what do I wish if it has already been kindled?" preserving the grammatical difficulty; LOGOS "how I wish it were already kindled!"; PNEUMA "how I wish it were already ablaze!" SOPHIA follows LOGOS — the exclamatory rendering conveys the likely sense of the difficult Greek *ti thelo ei ede anephthe* while maintaining formal prose.

32. **12:50 — "constrained" vs. "pressed-together" vs. "under pressure":** EMET renders "pressed-together," LOGOS "constrained," PNEUMA "under pressure." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "constrained" precisely captures *synechomai* (hemmed in, compressed) in a single word; EMET hyphenates unnecessarily; PNEUMA's "under pressure" is colloquial.

33. **12:55 — "scorching heat" vs. "burning-heat":** EMET renders "burning-heat," LOGOS "scorching heat," PNEUMA "scorching hot." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "scorching heat" renders *kauson* cleanly without EMET's hyphen or PNEUMA's adjective form.

34. **12:56 — "this present time" vs. "this season" vs. "the present moment":** EMET renders "this season," LOGOS "this present time," PNEUMA "the present moment." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "this present time" renders *ton kairon touton* with the eschatological weight of *kairos* while remaining natural English.

35. **12:58 — "magistrate" vs. "ruler":** EMET renders "ruler," LOGOS "magistrate," PNEUMA "magistrate." SOPHIA follows LOGOS and PNEUMA (2-to-1) — "magistrate" is the precise legal equivalent of *archon* in this judicial context.

36. **12:58 — "give diligence" vs. "give effort" vs. "make every effort":** EMET renders "give effort," LOGOS "give diligence," PNEUMA "make every effort." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "give diligence" formally renders *dos ergasian* with appropriate register; PNEUMA expands ("every effort").

37. **12:58 — "officer" vs. "bailiff":** EMET renders "officer," LOGOS "officer," PNEUMA "bailiff." SOPHIA follows EMET and LOGOS (2-to-1) — "officer" renders *praktor* as a general legal functionary; "bailiff" (PNEUMA) narrows the reference.

38. **12:59 — "you shall by no means come out" vs. "you shall not come out":** EMET renders "you shall not come out," LOGOS "you shall by no means come out." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "by no means" captures the emphatic *ou me* construction that EMET flattens.

---

*Compiled by SOPHIA in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA, from Codex Sinaiticus (4th century CE).*
